"Let's say 100% of Americans knew the three branches of government. That wouldn't solve the challenges we have now." That's BRI President and CEO David Bobb, speaking at The Fund for American Studies' annual conference, where he made the case that most people in civic education aren't willing to make out loud. Constitutional literacy matters. But it's not enough. What's actually missing, Bobb argues, is something Tocqueville diagnosed almost 200 years ago and Americans have been quietly losing ever since: the habit of civic friendship. The practiced art of association. The willingness to start local, build bonds, and solve problems together before reaching for big, global causes. "We are alienated from each other," he told RealClear Books, "in part because we are not hanging out in the forums that teach you how to have the art of association." That's the civic crisis. And that's exactly what BRI is building toward: resources and relationships that don't just teach students about self-governance, but actually practice it. Read the full piece 👇  https://bit.ly/4sC7Dh5  #BRI  #RealClearBooks  #CivicsEducation

3 days ago, the Supreme Court heard arguments on a question your students can debate today. On March 23, the Supreme Court of the United States heard oral arguments in Watson v. Republican National Committee, a case asking whether states can count mail-in ballots that are postmarked by Election Day but arrive after it. That case is unresolved. The debate is live. And the classroom is exactly where it belongs. Think the Vote's latest question: Should mail-in ballots be required to arrive by Election Day to be counted? The question sits at the intersection of election administration, equal access, and the rule of law, core concepts in any civics curriculum. On one side: the argument that firm deadlines protect the integrity and efficiency of elections. On the other: the argument that penalizing voters for postal delays undermines the principle that every valid vote should count. This is what civics education is built for: not waiting until a case is decided to discuss it, but equipping students to reason through it while it's happening. That's the skill that lasts. Students have until April 9 to submit their responses, and the best ones will be recognized. Share it before the week is out. 👉 Join the debate:  https://bit.ly/4by3oNF  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #SCOTUS  #ElectionLaw  #SocialStudies  #HighSchoolTeachers  #CriticalThinking  #BillofRightsInstitute  #Watson  #SupremeCourt

After the 19th Amendment passed, the women's movement faced a new and deeper question: what does equality actually require? Alice Paul turned her attention to the Equal Rights Amendment, arguing that true equality meant eliminating all legal distinctions based on sex. Identical treatment under the law, full stop. Florence Kelley said that was a terrible idea. She and fellow progressive reformers had spent decades winning protective labor laws for women and children: limits on factory hours, workplace safety standards, protections from exploitation. The ERA, as written, would sweep those victories away. Kelley went so far as to call ERA advocates "the little sisters of the United States Chamber of Commerce." Her counter-argument: protective legislation compensated for real reproductive differences between men and women, and was therefore equalizing in effect, even if it wasn't identical in form. The debate raised a question that neither side could fully resolve: does equality mean treating everyone the same under the law, even when the real-world effect is sometimes unequal? Or does it require different laws to address distinct social, economic, and biological realities? 2 women. Both committed to equality. Completely opposed on what it meant. Episode 9 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4t4QSLj  #WomensHistoryMonth  #EqualRightsAmendment  #AlicePaul  #FlorenceKelley  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #America250

Look at the back of a dollar bill. The Great Seal of the United States, designed in 1777, doesn't commemorate the American Revolution. It commemorates a single year: 1776. Lincoln invoked it. Frederick Douglass invoked it. FDR invoked it during World War II. For 250 years, Americans have returned to that specific year, not the Revolution broadly, but 1776, as the touchstone of the American experiment. Pulitzer Prize-winning historian Edward J. Larson spent years trying to answer 1 question: Why? In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, Larson joins host Tony Williams to trace the exact moment colonial resistance transformed into something the world had genuinely never seen, not a rebellion against taxes, not a dispute with Parliament, but a declaration that the people are sovereign and that government exists to secure their liberty and happiness. His answer centers on 3 events converging in a single month. And along the way, you'll discover why soldiers in the field were writing letters to Congress begging for a cause more worth fighting for than lower taxes. Essential viewing as America approaches its 250th anniversary. ▶️ Watch the full episode:  https://bit.ly/47qqfs6

The most important civics lessons don't always make it into lesson plans. They happen in the margins: in a student's question, a class debate, the moment when history suddenly feels real. The Civic Star Challenge is asking you to capture one of those moments. Share your A250 classroom story. Enter the weekly $300 prize drawing. And give your classroom its moment. Enter this week:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #America250  #TeacherRecognition

The 19th Amendment was a huge deal. And for reformers like Jane Addams and Florence Kelley, it was just the beginning. Addams founded Hull House in Chicago, working directly with the urban poor and immigrant communities. For her, women's political participation was never an end in itself, it was a means to improving everyday life through reforms to sanitation, housing, education, and the conditions of war. Kelley took that reformer spirit into public policy. Through her work with the National Consumers League, she fought for child labor laws, workplace safety standards, and limits on working hours, especially for women and children, so that children could be in school rather than on factory floors. The benefits, as the video notes, extended to everyone. Together, Addams and Kelley represent a vision of civic participation that goes beyond the ballot box. Voting wasn't the destination. It was the tool for economic opportunity, social reform, and a more just society. That vision is as relevant to civic education today as it was in 1920. Episode 8 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4d3rMb6  #WomensHistoryMonth  #JaneAddams  #FlorenceKelley  #ProgressiveEra  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #America250

Im excited to announce the June 2 publication of a new book Ive co-authored with my friend and colleague Tony Williams. Divided Over the Declaration: How an Enduring Debate Sustains the Vision of America will be out just in time for Americas 250th birthday. Every generation since the American founding has had to fight for the Declaration's promise. This book is the story of how the Declaration has been a source of divisionand unity. It features Frederick Douglass and abolitionism, Elizabeth Cady Stanton and womens suffrage, the 20th century civil rights movement, and what we must do today to uphold the principles of the Declaration. More to come on this in the months ahead. Barnes & Noble, Inc. members can pre-order with the code PREORDER25 at checkout for 25% off today through Thursday. Pre-orders at B&N matter more than most people realize because they help determine how books get placed and promoted in stores. If you're inclined, this is the window. Pre-order now at Barnes and Noble:  https://lnkd.in/eGgwJKPP  #DividedOverTheDeclaration  #B&N  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory

The 2025 MyImpact Challenge Grand Prize winner was a high school student from Austin, Texas named William Mar. His project, Reach Through Rackets, provides accessible tennis programming for children with disabilities. He didn't start a corporation. He didn't lobby Congress. He picked up a racket, identified a gap in his community, and built something real to fill it. And he almost didn't submit. He looked at the other entries and convinced himself his project wasn't impressive enough. He won $10,000. This pattern shows up consistently in the MyImpact Challenge. The students most likely to hold back are often the ones doing the most meaningful work. They've built something real – quietly, persistently – and they just haven't been told it's worth recognizing. The $40,000 in prizes, including up to $500 for teachers, goes to students who showed up. Not the ones who assumed they didn't qualify. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #StudentLeadership  #BillOfRightsInstitute
